methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $80M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Avalanche Energy is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Robin Langtry and Brian Riordan that has raised $80M to date. The broader Climate & Energy sector contains 9 peer companies across public and private markets, including one public comparable (LNZA) and eight private peers such as Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen.

The valuation analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, the only applicable triangulation approach given data constraints. Using $80M in total capital raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $160M. Peer-median triangulation was not viable; while nine same-sector comparables exist, none of the narrow filters—same stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier—produced the minimum three datapoints required for statistical reliability. Drawing from the full-sector median would introduce uncontrolled selection bias given the heterogeneity of business models and development stages within climate tech.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $136M (low) to $184M (high), with a median point estimate of $160M. This range reflects the single-method constraint and assumes the 2.0x progression multiple is appropriate for Avalanche's risk profile and market conditions. Key uncertainties include the absence of tightly matched private-market comparables at the same stage and valuation tier, which limits external validation of the stage-progression assumption.
